Empires & Puzzles Raul Review: The Lovesick Nobleman of the Opera

Raul brings the drama of the Opera to Empires & Puzzles, but does his performance earn a standing ovation or rotten tomatoes? We analyze his controversial 'Ballad of Attraction' and discover a hidden mechanic that might just save his career.
Raul: The Ballad of the Benchwarmer?
Raul: The Lovesick Nobleman Who Might Just Steal Your Buffs (If Not Your Heart)
Raul is a 4-star Fire (Red) hero from the

challenge event. As a Fighter class hero, he comes with the innate ability to revive, which fits the dramatic, "the show must go on" theme of the Opera family. Released alongside heavy hitters like Christine Daae and Phantom of the Opera, Raul often feels like the understudy who was thrust onto the stage before he was fully rehearsed.

While his damage output has been widely criticized by the community, Raul possesses a unique mechanical quirk in his special skill that allows him to perform a magic trick few other heroes can: making "undispellable" buffs disappear.

Raul captures the essence of a romantic lead in a tragic opera. With his flowing white shirt, dramatic pose, and ponytail that defies gravity, he looks ready to burst into a passionate aria at any moment. The art style fits the

aesthetic perfectly—theatrical, slightly exaggerated, and very polished. He doesn't look like a warrior; he looks like he's about to challenge you to a dance-off, which, considering his special skill, is exactly what he does.
When we look at Raul's stats through the lens of the modern meta, the picture is a bit mixed.
This is actually quite respectable for a 4-star hero. It's high enough to make his tile damage relevant in stack teams.
This is where the tragedy begins. 629 is perilously low by modern standards, making him extremely squishy.
Also on the lower side compared to the 1400+ HP behemoths we see in newer families like the Constructs or Goblins.
In short, Raul is a glass cannon who forgot to bring the cannon. He relies heavily on his Fighter class revive and passive healing to stay alive long enough to finish his song.
is a complex skill with a very polarizing reputation.
Let's be blunt—this is negligible. At average speed, hitting three targets for 120% is essentially a tickle. Do not bring Raul for his damage.

The target and nearby enemies are forced to dance, suffering -20% mana generation and -20% healing received. While useful, these percentages are quite low compared to other dedicated controllers like Proteus or even standard mana cutters like Li Xiu.
Preventing new status effects is powerful, essentially acting as a buff block.
"All existing status effects will be removed when this effect is added."

Note the wording—it does not say "dispelled." In Empires & Puzzles mechanics, "removing" or "replacing" an effect often bypasses the "Undispellable" tag. This means Raul can theoretically wipe clean buffs that standard dispellers (like Sabina or Melendor) cannot touch, provided the target is not immune to ailments entirely. This is his primary niche.
Average (10 tiles). With a lvl 23 mana troop, you can get this down to 9 tiles.
With 750 Attack, a 120% hit deals roughly 900 damage
defense reduction. Against modern 4-star defenses (800+ Def), this will likely result in hits of 150-200 damage per hero. It's basically a scratch.
Heals 10% HP when an ailment expires. If he has 1200 HP, that's a 120 HP heal. It provides some sustain but won't save him from a sniper shot.
Family Bonus, Passives, Aether Power & Unique Abilities
The Opera family is designed to be self-sustaining, punishing enemies who rely on ailments.
The stat boost (Attack/Defense/HP) is standard but always welcome, helping patch up his flimsy defense if you run him with other Opera heroes.
This is excellent. In wars where ailments fly constantly (burns, defense down), Raul can passively regenerate health just by surviving the duration or being cleansed.
This ensures his tile damage remains consistent, even against attack-down heroes like Scarlet or Skittleskull.
While more damage is usually good, on a hero with a 120% damage multiplier, this feels wasted. A defensive Aether Power like
would have served him much better given his low survivability.

Upon release, Raul was immediately overshadowed by his 5-star counterparts. The community quickly identified his low damage and average speed as major drawbacks, earning him the reputation of a "benchwarmer."
(Construct family) who boasts significantly higher stats and impactful minions, or
classics, Raul feels dated. His stats are fragile, and his impact-per-tile is low.

. He is not a general-purpose hero. You will not use him for farming, and you likely won't use him in standard raid attacks. However, in specific scenarios involving undispellable buffs (like certain "Slime" passives or specific event bosses), he can be a skeleton key.
Raul needs to survive to cast his special. If he dies before he sings, he does nothing.
Patches his critical weakness (low Defense). Maximizes the value of his Fighter revive (reviving with more HP potential).
His tile damage won't be as high, but his special skill damage was never going to be good anyway.
pushes his Attack stat near 900, making his red tiles hit very hard.

. Treat him as a utility support, not an attacker.
Can remove status effects that are otherwise undispellable by replacing them with the Dance effect.
Prevents enemies from gaining new buffs for 3 turns.
Self-healing passive adds durability in ailment-heavy matches.
The chance to revive can win wars.
120% hit is one of the lowest in the 4-star tier.
Average speed is too slow for a controller with such low impact.
Low base defense makes him an easy target.
The dance only lasts 3 turns, meaning the control window is very tight.
At Very Fast speed, his ability to lock down 3 enemies and block buffs becomes oppressive.
He prevents the enemy from gaining buffs, neutralizing the tournament rule.
Against heroes with undispellable buffs (e.g., Slime Revive, Taunt heroes if not immune to ailments).
In Rush, Raul prevents the enemy from buffing up (countering Boril/Cyprian tanks) while Colen and Wilbur decimate the team.
Mist slows mana and blocks buffs; Raul also blocks buffs and cuts mana gen. Alternating their fires can keep an enemy team permanently unable to gain buffs.
Enemies who resist ailments will resist the Dance entirely, meaning no damage, no debuff, and NO DISPEL.
Since his "dispel" is actually an ailment application, heroes who block ailments (like
) completely shut him down. He cannot remove their immunity block because the block prevents his "removal" mechanic from landing.

If you lack a deep roster, Raul can serve as a functional red support. His high attack stat helps with titans (for tile damage), and his buff block is useful against annoying bosses. However, do not prioritize him over staples like

Raul is a luxury tool. He is worth keeping at 3/60 or maxing one copy purely for the "removal" mechanic to counter specific future meta threats that rely on undispellable buffs. Do not Limit Break him unless you find yourself using him constantly in wars. He is not worth Alpha Aethers.
The community reception to Raul was chilly, to say the least.
"He feels weak... miserable 120% damage to three at average speed."
However, savvy players noted his potential as a counter: "He removes undispellable stuff... You can take that slime revive status away from Gurick."
The general consensus: "He does a lot of things... none of which very well."
Raul is the definition of a "toolbox hero." He isn't the hammer, and he isn't the screwdriver. He's that weird, bent piece of metal you keep in the bottom of the drawer because it's the

thing that can open that one specific type of battery compartment. You won't use him often, but on the rare occasion you face a team full of undispellable buffs, you'll be glad he's waiting in the wings.
Keep one, but don't give him a solo unless you have to.
A detailed review of Raul, the 4-star Fire hero from the Secrets of the Opera event. Analysis of his Ballad of Attraction, stats, and unique ability to counter undispellable buffs.
